channel.title: Career on Fengyu WANG
channel.description: Recent content in Career on Fengyu WANG

item.title: Workplace Survival to Entrepreneurship
item.description:
The transition from workplace survival to independent entrepreneurship is essentially an upgrade in cognition. What most people accumulate at work is the ability to do a job well, not the ability to run a business. These two require completely different skill structures.

The workplace survival phase is about building professional competence and reputation. You need to become more professional than most in one or two areas, so that the company feels it is worth keeping you. The strategy for this phase can be summarised as: use professional competence to earn job security, use professional reputation to earn room for growth.

item.title: Surface-Level Experience vs. Core Capabilities
item.description:
In the workplace, capability management can be strategically divided into two layers: surface-level experience and core capabilities. Use the surface layer to build trust, and protect the core layer as your competitive moat.

The first step is capabilitylayering. Separate what you can share from what you must never share. Surface-level experience includes general skills, industrycommon knowledge, publicly shareable methodologies, and commonwork experience. These can be freely shared. They help you build a professional image and earn the trust of colleagues and managers.

item.title: Premium Job Consulting on the Wrong Platform
item.description:
Selling premium services on a low-price platform is essentially a channel mismatch. It is not that your service is bad. It is that you are selling the wrong thing in the wrong place.

Let us analyse the problem from a market positioning perspective.

Xianyu user profile determines its price range and usage scenario. People go to Xianyu to find bargains, second-hand goods, and deals. When a provider of premium consulting services appears on this platform, they naturally attract customers looking for low prices. There is a fundamental conflict between low-price customers and premium services. Premium services require clients to have a certain level oflevel of understanding and purchasing power to understand and appreciate their value, and the user base of a low-price platform statistically lacks these characteristics.

item.title: Stay Honest in a World That Prefers You Did Not
item.description:
Staying honest and clear-headed at work often earns you a strange label: being told you have low EQ, that you are too rigid. This feedback can make you doubt yourself, but the truth it reveals is exactly the opposite.

The people calling you low EQ are exactly the ones whose path to muddy waters you are blocking.

Break it down: when they say you have low EQ, what they really mean is you are too honest, too principled, too hard to fool, too hard to drag into their shady dealings.

item.title: A Bank Teller Existential Crisis
item.description:
The first symptom of an existential crisis is a disturbance in the perception of time. When someone has sat at the same window for six years, time is no longer a linear river. It becomes an unmarked expanse of water.

At ten to nine in the morning, he puts on his uniform, fastens the buttons, and glances at the mirror in the changing room. The person in the mirror wears a navy blue suit with a badge on the lapel, his name and an employee number. He knows that in ten minutes, that number will matter more than his name. He walks to the counter, sits down, turns on the computer, enters his password. The system boots up. At nine oclock, the shutter rises. This is his sixth year at this bank, at this window.

item.title: Three Core Corporate Governance Dilemmas
item.description:
Three common dilemmas in corporate governance all point to the same root cause: the mismatch between institutional design and human nature.

The first dilemma is perverse incentives. When a company has no reasonable workload boundaries or incentive mechanisms, the more an employee does, the more tasks they are given. Hard work is not rewarded; it is punished with more work. The result is a reverse incentive: those who work hard lose out, while those who coast are comfortable. Employees actively reduce their output and pretend to be busy, and overall efficiency declines.
